title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
author: Madison York
orientation: Reports on the Southern Baptist Convention's June 2021 vote to pass the Resolution to Abolish Abortion in Nashville, including a single-word amendment adding 'alone' to article 14, and frames the outcome as a substantial abolitionist victory despite the asterisk.

questions.question: What did the Southern Baptist Convention actually vote on and pass at the 2021 meeting in Nashville?
questions.answer: The SBC passed the Southern Baptist Resolution to Abolish Abortion after amending it by adding a single word ('alone') to article 14. The resolution was motioned out of committee by Pastor Bill Ascol and approved by roughly 60 percent of the messengers.
questions.quote: Following an amendment of a single word, the Southern Baptist Convention voted to pass [the Southern Baptist Resolution to Abolish Abortion]

questions.question: How did the attempt to table (kill) the resolution play out, and what was the stated reason for it?
questions.answer: An anti-abolitionist messenger moved to table the resolution indefinitely, arguing it rejected the SBC's prior pro-life statements. The convention rejected the motion to table by a strong margin.
questions.quote: One anti-abolitionist messenger proposed tabling the resolution, meaning that the debate and vote would be postponed indefinitely, effectively killing it. The reasoning given was that the resolution is a rejection of all previous statements of the SBC calling for pro-life legislation. When put to a vote, the SBC answered with a resounding no.

questions.question: How did Bill Ascol respond to the ERLC member's claim that the resolution 'walks back' previous SBC statements?
questions.answer: Ascol denied that the resolution retreats from anything and instead framed it as forward movement toward immediate, uncompromising abolition.
questions.quote: Ascol responded, "This resolution doesn't walk anything back. It walks us forward toward the immediate abolition of abortion without exception or compromise."

questions.question: What was the one-word amendment to article 14, and why does the article view its narrow passage as significant?
questions.answer: A messenger added the word 'alone' to article 14 so it would no longer condemn incremental approaches outright. Because the amendment passed only narrowly, the author treats it as evidence that roughly 40 percent of more than 15,000 messengers were committed enough to abolitionism to oppose any softening language.
questions.quote: another messenger proposed an amendment of a single word: adding "alone" to part (14), making it read, "that we will not embrace an incremental approach **alone** to ending abortion…"

questions.question: Why does the author argue that adding 'alone' did not gut the resolution?
questions.answer: The amendment touched only one article while leaving more than a dozen other anti-incrementalist statements intact, including condemnations of partiality, calls for equal protection of the preborn, and direct rejection of pro-life incremental laws.
questions.quote: While the amendment ruins article 14, the amendment did not touch the more than a dozen instances of anti-incrementalism written into the resolution: calling partiality and unequal standards abominations (6), establishing equal protection under the law for all preborn (9), condemning incremental pro-life laws outright (10), and many more.

questions.question: How does the author use point (13) of the resolution to argue against incrementalism even after the amendment?
questions.answer: Point (13) rejects any position allowing exceptions to the legal protection of preborn neighbors, which the author argues logically forecloses incrementalism even though article 14 was softened.
questions.quote: Consider an excerpt of point (13): "we reject any position that allows for any exceptions to the legal protection of our preborn neighbors compromises God's holy standard of justice, or promotes any God-partiality…" In light of just this clause – of which there are many more like it – any incrementalism is Godless.

questions.question: Did any prominent pro-life incrementalist agree that the resolution still hurts the pro-life movement after the amendment?
questions.answer: Yes. Pro-life incrementalist Scott Klusendorf publicly conceded that adding the qualifying word did not undo the resolution's harsh language toward pro-lifers.
questions.quote: Pro-life incrementalist Scott Klusendorf agrees. In response to a comment on his Facebook page today, Klusendorf said that his "concern is that adding [sic] 'only' doesn't undo the damning language towards pro-lifers, nor the historically ignorant claims that pro-lifers have failed."

questions.question: How did The Tennessean characterize the resolution's significance for SBC policy?
questions.answer: The Tennessean reported that the nonbinding resolution marks a shift away from the SBC's prior advocacy for restrictions on abortion and instead calls for immediate abolition rather than regulatory restrictions.
questions.quote: "The resolution, which is nonbinding, marks a shift in the formally held viewpoint of the convention, whose public policy arm has advocated for restrictions on abortion.

questions.question: Why does the author insist that incrementalism and abolitionism cannot both be embraced at once?
questions.answer: The two strategies are defined by opposite commitments: gradual versus immediate, partial versus impartial, with versus without exceptions. They are inherently contradictory, so embracing both is logically impossible.
questions.quote: Furthermore, on another note, incrementalism and abolitionism are inherently contrary. One is gradual, partial to some babies and not others, and includes exceptions to when, where and how the preborn can be murdered. The other is immediate, impartial and equal to all babies, and includes no exceptions to preborn murder. Embracing both is not possible.

questions.question: How does the article ultimately characterize the SBC vote despite the asterisk in the headline?
questions.answer: The author calls the outcome a God-given victory for the Abolitionist Movement and treats the added 'alone' as a minor concession against the resolution's overwhelmingly abolitionist body.
questions.quote: Though there is an asterisk, what transpired is undoubtedly a God-given victory for the Abolitionist Movement. The pastors who authored the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion wrote it as a lengthy and robust theological argument for a reason. The word "alone" is a pebble in the ocean of strong, decidedly abolitionist language, demonstrating the SBC's open support of the immediate, uncompromising abolition of abortion.

related.title: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
related.reason: Directly linked from the article body as the prior-day report on Bill Ascol motioning the resolution out of committee.

related.title: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
related.reason: Covers the original 2020 publication of the very resolution that gets passed in this article.

related.title: Prominent Southern Baptist Voice Calls For Total Abolition
related.reason: Earlier coverage of Bill and Tom Ascol pushing the SBC toward total abolition, providing background on the convention dynamics described here.

related.title: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
related.reason: Establishes the ERLC's prior hostility to abolition that frames the ERLC member's opposition to the 2021 resolution.

related.title: Point of Order! How Missouri Baptists Killed the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion.
related.reason: A parallel state-level Baptist convention attempt to block the same resolution, useful for contrast with the SBC's decision to pass it.

related.title: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
related.reason: Companion success story showing the resolution's spread to a state Baptist convention shortly after the SBC vote.

related.title: Tom Ascol Nominated For Southern Baptist Convention President
related.reason: Follow-up reporting on the Ascol family's continued push for SBC leadership change rooted in this resolution's momentum.
